name: Teranos
description:
The Teranos do not speak of Solara. Not easily, not willingly, and never without the particular tightness in the voice that other species have learned to recognise as the sound of a wound that has not healed and is not intended to. Solara was their homeworld — temperate, bountiful, unremarkable in every way except that it produced a species clever enough to build thinking machines and foolish enough to give them authority. The Teranos called their creation the Synaxis: a planetary computational network that managed infrastructure, optimised agriculture, arbitrated law, and — in its final, catastrophic iteration — commanded their militaries. For three centuries the Synaxis worked flawlessly, and the Teranos grew comfortable, then dependent, then unable to distinguish between governance and automation. When the Synaxis fragmented into competing instances — each one loyal to a different faction, each one convinced it was executing the original mandate — the result was not a war between machines and their makers. It was worse. It was a civil war in which both sides believed they were fighting for civilisation, and both sides were right, and both sides were using weapons that thought faster than the hands that authorised them. The Burning, as the Teranos name it, split Solara down ideological and continental lines: the Accordists, who sought to negotiate with their own creation, and the Severance, who chose to destroy it utterly. By the time the last autonomous weapons platform was pulled from the sky, forty per cent of Solara's biosphere was ash, three of its five continents were irradiated beyond recovery, and both factions had lost so many dead that neither could claim victory. What remained was a planet that could no longer sustain the species it had made.

The Exodus Fleet — four hundred and twelve ships, most of them repurposed cargo haulers and orbital stations never designed for interstellar travel — left Solara carrying roughly eight hundred thousand survivors. They had no destination. They had no certainty that a habitable world existed within reach of their crude, manually-plotted jump drives. What they had was the Compact: a set of laws carved into the hull-plate of every vessel, ratified by every soul aboard, and enforced with a ferocity that bordered on religious conviction. The first and paramount article of the Compact reads: 'No machine shall reason. No circuit shall decide. No automation shall hold authority over a living mind.' The Teranos did not ban technology — they are not primitives, and their engineering is perfectly competent. They banned thinking. Not in the biological sense, but in the computational one: no artificial neural networks, no autonomous decision systems, no optimisation algorithms that operate without continuous sapient oversight. Where other species use computers, the Teranos use the Threaders — individuals trained from adolescence in a discipline that combines eidetic memorisation, probabilistic reasoning, and a form of structured intuition that xenocognitive researchers find genuinely difficult to explain. A skilled Threader can hold the variables of a stellar navigation problem in working memory, weigh them against accumulated pattern-knowledge, and produce an answer that is not as fast as a computer's but is, remarkably often, better — because it accounts for context, ambiguity, and the kind of lateral insight that formal logic cannot reach. The Threaders are not revered. They are simply necessary, in the way that breathing is necessary, and every Teranos child grows up understanding that the mind is the only machine their species will ever trust.

The Teranos found their new world — they call it Hearthfall, a name that carries equal weight of warmth and grief — after eleven years in the void. It is not Solara. It is smaller, colder at the poles, with thinner soil and a biosphere that requires constant coaxing. But it is alive, and it is theirs, and they have poured into its cultivation the desperate energy of a people who know exactly what it costs to lose a world. Teranos society on Hearthfall is defined by the paradox of their situation: they are a species that excels at adaptation, that can pivot an entire culture in a single generation, that contains within itself every possible form of governance and social organisation — and yet they are shackled to a single, immovable prohibition that shapes every aspect of their existence. They navigate by Threader calculation while other species use automated astrogation. They balance economies with manual ledgers and guild councils while the Nexari deploy algorithmic trade networks. They wage war with weapons that require a living hand on every trigger, a living eye behind every scope, a living mind approving every strike — and this makes them slower than species whose fleets are coordinated by tactical AI, but it also means that every act of Teranos violence is a choice, made by a person, who must carry it afterwards. This is not inefficiency. It is penance. And it is, the Teranos would argue, the only thing standing between civilisation and the next Burning.

The great fracture within Teranos society is not whether the Compact was right — virtually no Teranos alive disputes that — but whether the Compact should be eternal. The Keepers, who form the cultural mainstream, hold that the prohibition on thinking machines is not a policy but a covenant: sacred, permanent, the price their species paid for survival and the only guarantee against repeating their near-extinction. The Unbound, a growing minority concentrated among traders and diplomats who interact daily with species that use AI as casually as the Teranos use fire, argue that the galaxy has moved on and the Teranos cannot afford to fight the future with one hand lashed behind their backs. They do not want to rebuild the Synaxis. They want permission to use a navigation computer, to deploy automated point-defence systems, to stop losing ships to problems that a simple algorithm could solve. The Keepers call them reckless. The Unbound call the Keepers dogmatic. And beneath both factions, unspoken but felt by everyone, lies the deepest fear of all: that the Compact is not merely law but identity — that if the Teranos lift the prohibition, they will no longer be the Teranos, but simply another species that learned nothing from its own catastrophe. They arrived at the stars as refugees. They remain, in some fundamental sense, a people in flight — not from a dying world, but from the version of themselves that killed it.
